figures.caption: Fig. 1. The proposed Degradation-aware Adaptive Context Gating framework for unified image restoration. layer prompts guide the attention mechanism, while global feature perform frequency-domain modulation. A dual-path gating mechanism in the spatial and channel domains adaptively filters degradation noise, enabling robust restoration under diverse adverse conditions.

TABLE VII ABLATION STUDY OF AGF INTEGRATED INTO REPRESENTATIVE U-SHAPED-BASED IMAGE RESTORATION MODELS. QUANTITATIVE COMPARISONS ARE CONDUCTED ON DEHAZING, DERAINING, AND DENOISING TASKS. FLOPS ARE COMPUTED WITH AN INPUT RESOLUTION OF 224×224.

TABLE I PERFORMANCE COMPARISON BETWEEN THE PROPOSED DACG-IR AND STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS ON three DEGRADATION TASKS.THE BEST AND SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD AND UNDERLINE, RESPECTIVELY.

TABLE II PERFORMANCE COMPARISON BETWEEN THE PROPOSED DACG-IR AND STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS ON five DEGRADATION TASKS.THE BEST AND SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD AND UNDERLINE, RESPECTIVELY.

TABLE VI PERFORMANCE METRICS ON THE ALL-WEATHER IMAGE RESTORATION BENCHMARK. RESULTS ARE REPORTED IN PSNR (DB) AND SSIM, WITH BOLD AND UNDERLINED VALUES DENOTING THE TOP-PERFORMING AND RUNNER-UP METHODS, RESPECTIVELY.
TABLE III QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON ON CDD11 [54] UNDER SINGLE, DOUBLE AND TRIPLE DEGRADATION SETTINGS. EACH ENTRY REPORTS PSNR / SSIM.
TABLE IV THE PROPOSED DACG-IR AND STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS ON REPRESENTATIVE SINGLE-DEGRADATION RESTORATION BENCHMARKS.

Fig. 4. Visual comparison under the all-in-one setting with five degradation types. Compared with representative methods, including PromptIR [24], InstructIR [35], and DFPIR [19], DACG-IR produces visually superior restoration results with clearer structures and higher perceptual quality. Zoom in for better view.
